---
title: "Exercise 5 — Push Your Local Repo to GitHub"
format: html
---

**Module 4 | ~25 minutes**

---

## Goal

Connect your local repository to GitHub and push your work online.

---

## Prerequisite

Test your SSH key:

```bash
ssh -T git@github.com
# Expected: Hi YOUR_USERNAME! You've successfully authenticated.
```

If this fails, see [Pre-course Setup](../qmd/precourse_setup.qmd).

---

## Part A — Create the GitHub repository

1. Go to [https://github.com/new](https://github.com/new)
2. Name it `experiment-tracker` (same as your local folder)
3. Set to **Public**
4. **Do NOT** initialize with a README (your local repo already has files)
5. Click **Create repository**

Copy the **SSH** URL shown on the next page.

---

## Part B — Connect and push

```bash
cd ~/Downloads/experiment-tracker

# Add GitHub as the remote
git remote add origin git@github.com:YOUR_USERNAME/experiment-tracker.git

# Verify
git remote -v

# Push (and set upstream)
git push -u origin main
```

---

## Part C — Verify on GitHub

1. Go to `https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/experiment-tracker`
2. `samples.csv`, `analysis_log.md`, `results_summary.md` and their commit history should be there
3. Click **commits** to explore the history
4. Click a commit hash to see exactly what changed

---

## Part D — Edit on GitHub, pull locally

1. On GitHub, click **results_summary.md** → pencil icon
2. Add a line: `Reviewed by collaborator on GitHub.`
3. Commit: `added review note on GitHub`

Now pull that change:

```bash
git pull
cat results_summary.md    # new line is here
```

---

## Check your understanding

**Question:** What would have happened if you had a **local, uncommitted**
edit to `results_summary.md` when you ran `git pull` just now?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Click to reveal the answer

`git pull` would likely refuse, with a message like
`error: Your local changes to the following files would be overwritten by merge`.
Git protects you from silently losing uncommitted work. You'd need to either
commit your changes first, or temporarily set them aside with `git stash`,
pull, then `git stash pop` to bring them back.
:::

**Question:** You used `git push -u origin main` the first time, but just
`git push` every time after. Why does the first push need the extra flag?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Click to reveal the answer

`-u` (short for `--set-upstream`) tells Git "remember that my local `main`
branch tracks `origin/main`." Once that link is recorded, plain `git push`
and `git pull` know where to send/fetch without you specifying the remote
and branch every time.
:::

---

## Part E — Full cycle

```bash
nano analysis_log.md      # add a "Step 4 — Visualization" note locally
git add analysis_log.md
git commit -m "added visualization step"
git push

# Check GitHub — your change appears immediately
```

---

## What you learned

| Command | What it does |
|---------|-------------|
| `git remote add origin <url>` | Connect to GitHub |
| `git remote -v` | Show connections |
| `git push -u origin main` | Push and set upstream |
| `git push` | Push new commits |
| `git pull` | Fetch + merge from GitHub |
